text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

Samuel Turner, DOB: 22-07-1992, Hospital No: U914725 NHS No. 1085639742  
Flat 3, 48 Northside Gardens, London, NW5 6QD 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with vagus nerve stimulator (VNS) in situ/high ambient-noise workplace exposure with frequent task-switching/midday rest breaks often interrupted/migraine without aura/possible sleep fragmentation. 

Medication: Sodium Valproate 500 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 10 mg at night.  
Midazolam oromucosal 10 mg PRN as rescue per protocol.  
Sumatriptan 50 mg PRN for migraine. 

I reviewed Sam in clinic today. He works in early-years childcare, a busy environment with high background noise, frequent interruptions, and rapid multitasking. He reports that planned midday quiet breaks are often curtailed by staffing needs. He feels generally well between events but notes that fatigue accumulates toward the end of the week. He denies new head injuries, intercurrent infections, or recent medication side effects. Appetite is stable, weight unchanged. Sleep is variable; he wakes once or twice most nights due to residual noise at home but returns to sleep quickly. No concerns about mood, though he reports occasional work-related stress on busy days. 

Seizures: Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two to three days. Rescue medication was required once in June and once in August. The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review. He identifies noise peaks, abrupt transitions between tasks, and shortened rest breaks as likely precipitating factors. He is confident with his VNS magnet use and follows his agreed rescue plan at work, which has been communicated to his manager. There have been no injuries, tongue bites, or prolonged post-ictal states. 

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. No focal neurological deficits. Gait normal. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. VNS generator site well-healed, no local tenderness. 

Investigations: No recent bloods were available today. Last valproate level (six months ago) within therapeutic range. MRI brain (previous) unchanged. ECG last year normal. 

Plan:  
- Generalised epilepsy: No change to antiseizure medication at present given stable pattern and minimal rescue use. Continue current valproate and clobazam doses. Maintain existing VNS parameters; continue magnet swiping as trained.  
- Workplace measures: Patient will discuss protected midday quiet time with employer to reduce potential triggers related to interrupted breaks and noise peaks; he feels comfortable initiating this conversation.  
- Safety: Continue current rescue protocol at work; ensure midazolam in-date and accessible.  
- Monitoring: Arrange routine bloods via GP (FBC, LFTs, U&E, valproate level) within the next month.  
- Follow-up: Review in 6 months, sooner if cluster frequency increases, rescue is needed more than once per month, or any new features emerge. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two to three days.

Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two to three days. Rescue medication was required once in June and once in August. The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states that seizures are occurring in clusters every two to three days. The number of seizures within each cluster is not specified. Since the time-based frequency of events (clusters) is given but per-cluster count is unknown, normalize as 1 event per 2 to 3 days.
seizure_frequency_number: Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two to three days.
reference: Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two to three days. Rescue medication was required once in June and once in August. The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review
